# AI-Driven Performance Analysis: CEL vs Akamai

> Generated by Claude claude-opus-4-6 on 2026-03-02T12:45:00Z
> Run: 2026-03-02T10-32-10 | Duration: 30m | Event limit: 50000

## Executive Verdict

The native Akamai input outperforms the CEL input by **3.8× in throughput** (16,161 EPS vs 4,243 EPS) with **88–90% lower memory usage** (21.9 MB vs 191.5 MB avg alloc; 121 MB vs 636 MB RSS), while HTTP round-trip latency is statistically identical between the two inputs (~465 ms mean). The throughput gap is not caused by API limitations — both inputs see the same network latency — but by CEL's per-cycle evaluation overhead of ~8.4 seconds per request, which throttles polling to roughly one request per 9 seconds. Recommendation: migrate to the native Akamai input for production deployments handling sustained event streams; the CEL input is unsuitable for workloads where offset TTL (120s) is close to the polling interval.

## Throughput Analysis

The monitoring log and `/inputs/` counters are closely aligned for both runs, confirming no significant event loss at the pipeline layer:

| Source | CEL | Akamai |
|--------|-----|--------|
| /inputs/ events_published | 7,100,143 | 27,100,000 |
| Monitoring log events_published | 7,128,434 | 27,150,000 |
| Avg EPS | 4,243 | 16,161 |

The small delta (~28k CEL, ~50k Akamai) between monitoring and /inputs/ counters reflects events ACKed by the output after the final /inputs/ snapshot — expected behaviour with file output.

The Akamai input consistently received **50,000 events per batch** (the configured `event_limit`) across all 542 requests, indicating the API had more events available than the client was consuming. CEL made only **144 requests** in the same 30-minute window — one every ~12.5 seconds — leaving the vast majority of available API capacity unused. Both inputs were therefore bottlenecked by their own processing overhead, not by API server delivery rate.

## Latency Deep Dive

HTTP round-trip time is effectively identical:

| Percentile | CEL | Akamai | Δ |
|------------|-----|--------|---|
| mean | 469 ms | 464 ms | −1.2% |
| p99 | 696 ms | 598 ms | −14% |

This is the apples-to-apples signal: **both inputs experience the same network and API server latency**. The 14% p99 advantage for Akamai is within normal variance for sequential runs and does not indicate a structural difference.

The throughput gap is entirely explained by CEL's `cel_processing_time`:

- **CEL cel_processing_time mean: 8,886 ms** — this is the full cycle cost: HTTP RTT (469 ms) + JSON body parsing + CEL program evaluation + cursor state update + EdgeGrid HMAC signing. Subtracting HTTP RTT gives ~8.4 s of pure CEL overhead per request.
- **Akamai request_processing_time: 0 ms** — the native input's Go code for decoding and publishing events is too fast to register in the histogram (sub-millisecond), confirming that native Go parsing has negligible overhead compared to CEL expression evaluation.

Batch processing time (receipt → pipeline ACK) is faster for Akamai at median but has a higher p99 spike (4,907 ms vs 3,257 ms for CEL), consistent with the `growing_pipeline_active` warning: occasional backpressure bursts when the pipeline absorbs large batches faster than the file output can drain them.

## Memory and CPU Analysis

Memory usage reflects the structural difference between CEL's allocation-heavy evaluation model and Akamai's native path:

| Metric | CEL | Akamai | Δ |
|--------|-----|--------|---|
| Avg alloc | 191.5 MB | 21.9 MB | −88.6% |
| Max alloc | 308.2 MB | 30.0 MB | −90.3% |
| Avg RSS | 636 MB | 121 MB | −81.0% |

CEL's heap is 9× larger on average. This is not a leak — it is the expected consequence of CEL's evaluation model: each cycle allocates a JSON decode buffer (bytes.growSlice), constructs CEL value objects (`ConvertToType`), and publishes 50,000 events before the GC can reclaim them. The large heap headroom (636 MB RSS vs 308 MB alloc) is Go's GC allowing headroom before triggering a collection.

GC overhead in the CPU profile is **below the 20% threshold for both inputs** (CEL 0.0%, Akamai 0.1%), which appears to contradict the memory picture. This is explained by the CPU profile being a 30-second window at the run midpoint: CEL spends most of its midpoint window waiting (signaling goroutines) rather than actively evaluating or GC-ing. GC pauses are brief but the heap cost accumulates across cycles.

Akamai uses slightly more CPU by total milliseconds (65,379 ms/min vs 54,500 ms/min) because it is processing 3.8× more events per minute through the pipeline and output stages, yet its normalized load (0.26 vs 0.34) is lower — it is doing more work more efficiently.

## pprof Interpretation

**CEL CPU profile (midpoint 30s):**
The top function is `runtime.pthread_cond_signal` at 36.6% flat — Go's goroutine wake signal. At this point in the run CEL was in its waiting phase between requests (the 8.9s cycle is mostly blocking in CEL evaluation and I/O, not spinning). `runtime.pcvalue` (11.3%) and `runtime.step` (4.4%) are stack unwinding costs for GC metadata lookups, confirming background GC activity. `runtime.madvise` (4.3%) and `runtime.markrootSpans` (2.9%) are GC sweep operations — the GC is actively reclaiming the large batches. No CEL library functions appear in the flat top-10, which means the 30s window happened to fall in the inter-request idle phase.

**Akamai CPU profile (midpoint 30s):**
Even more idle-dominated: `runtime.pthread_cond_signal` 59.0%, `syscall.syscall` 19.3%, `runtime.pthread_cond_wait` 9.3%. The Akamai input spends nearly 90% of CPU time in goroutine coordination and syscall waits. This confirms the input is **network/API-bound**: the vast majority of elapsed time is waiting for the API to respond, not processing events. Worker utilization of 0.81% is consistent — 1 worker is active less than 1% of the time, mostly blocked on HTTP.

**CEL heap growth (baseline → final):**
The two dominant growth paths are `bytes.growSlice` (40.5%) and `github.com/google/cel-go/common/types.Bytes.ConvertToType` (39.8%). Together they account for 80% of heap growth and trace the same call chain: CEL reads the API response body into a growing `bytes.Buffer`, then crosses the CEL/Go value boundary via `ConvertToType` to make it accessible within the CEL expression. This is the core allocation cost of the CEL input — unavoidable without replacing the input type.

**Akamai heap growth (baseline → final):**
Growth is dominated by one-time initialization allocations: `libbeat/asset.GetFields` (24.2%), APM tracer setup, queue initialization. The actual event processing path (`akamai.createBeatEvent` 3.0%, `akamai.StreamEvents` 2.5%) contributes minimally, confirming that Akamai's per-event allocation is small and bounded.

**Block/mutex contention:**
Both inputs show identical contention patterns: `runtime.selectgo` ~91% (goroutines blocked on channel selects — expected for a pipeline architecture), `runtime.chanrecv1` ~8.8%. No input-specific lock contention is visible. The `growing_pipeline_active` issue in Akamai is a pipeline-level phenomenon, not a lock contention issue.

## Akamai Reliability Assessment

| Indicator | Count | Assessment |
|-----------|-------|------------|
| offset_expired | 0 | Clean — no offset TTL violations |
| offset_ttl_drops | 1 | One proactive drop — normal, handled gracefully |
| hmac_refresh | 0 | No clock skew between client and API server |
| cursor_drops | 0 | No window replays |
| api_400_fatal | 0 | No data loss |

The reliability picture is clean. The single `offset_ttl_drops` event is expected: when the input proactively detects that an offset is approaching its 120s TTL (e.g., after a slow request), it drops and re-fetches rather than risking a 416. This is correct behaviour and does not indicate instability.

Worker utilization of 0.81% with 1 worker confirms the current configuration is adequate for this throughput level. The test environment shows no signs of clock drift or API server instability that could bias the throughput comparison.

## Root Cause Hypotheses

1. **CEL evaluation overhead is the primary throughput bottleneck.** CEL's `cel_processing_time` of 8.9s per cycle (vs 464ms HTTP RTT) means 94% of each polling cycle is spent in expression evaluation, JSON parsing, and value boundary crossing — not waiting for the API. Evidence: `bytes.growSlice` and `Bytes.ConvertToType` together account for 80% of CEL heap growth; `cel_processing_time` is 19× `http_round_trip_time`.

2. **Akamai's `growing_pipeline_active` is caused by burst absorption, not a structural leak.** The native input publishes 50,000-event batches faster than the file output can drain them, causing transient pipeline queue buildup. Evidence: `batch_processing_time` p99 spikes to 4,907ms vs a mean of 2,625ms; total_failed and total_dropped are both 0, confirming no events were lost.

3. **CEL's high RSS (636 MB) is driven by large per-batch heap headroom, not a memory leak.** The GC allows the heap to grow between collections when the mutator (CEL evaluation) allocates faster than the collector can reclaim. Evidence: `max_alloc` (308 MB) vs `avg_alloc` (191 MB) shows significant intra-run variance consistent with GC cycles; heap growth paths trace to ephemeral evaluation buffers, not long-lived data structures.

4. **CEL's lower average CPU (54,500 ms/min) does not mean it is more efficient — it means it is less busy.** CEL is idle between its infrequent requests (one every 12.5s). Akamai processes 3.8× more events and therefore runs more pipeline/output code, but at lower normalized load. Evidence: Akamai avg load 0.26 vs CEL 0.34, despite doing 3.8× more work.

## Recommendations

1. **Migrate to the native Akamai input for production.** The 3.8× throughput advantage and 88% memory reduction are structural — they stem from eliminating CEL evaluation overhead entirely. No tuning of the CEL input can close this gap.

2. **Increase Akamai worker count to 2–4 to absorb burst backpressure.** The `growing_pipeline_active` warning indicates the single worker occasionally cannot drain the pipeline fast enough. Adding workers will reduce p99 batch_processing_time spikes. Try: `--akamai-workers 2`.

3. **Validate that the Akamai input's 50,000 events/batch does not exceed offset TTL.** With a 120s offset TTL and batch_processing_time mean of 2,625ms, the input is well within safe margins. However, under higher-latency network conditions, add margin: set `offset_ttl: 90s` in the Akamai config to trigger proactive drops earlier.

4. **For any remaining CEL usage, reduce `event_limit` to 10,000–20,000** to shorten CEL evaluation time per cycle (fewer events to parse and convert), which may help stay within the 120s offset TTL window under adverse conditions.

5. **Run a 60-minute test with monitoring snapshots every 5 minutes** to confirm that Akamai's heap growth is bounded (initialization allocations plateau). The current 30-minute run shows healthy patterns but a longer run would confirm the `growing_pipeline_active` issue does not worsen over time.

## Suggested Follow-up Tests

```bash
# 1. Multi-worker Akamai test: confirm growing_pipeline_active resolves with 2 workers
./bin/run_tests.sh \
    --akamai-url "https://host.akamai.com" \
    --client-token "..." --client-secret "..." --access-token "..." \
    --duration 30m --interval 1m --initial-interval 1h --event-limit 50000 \
    --akamai-workers 2 --akamai-batch-size 1000
# Expect: growing_pipeline_active warning disappears; batch_processing_time p99 drops

# 2. Long-run stability test: confirm Akamai heap growth is bounded over time
./bin/run_tests.sh \
    --akamai-url "https://host.akamai.com" \
    --client-token "..." --client-secret "..." --access-token "..." \
    --duration 60m --interval 1m --initial-interval 1h --event-limit 50000 \
    --akamai-workers 1 --akamai-batch-size 1000
# Expect: heap_final - heap_baseline growth rate similar to 30m run (not doubling)

# 3. Reduced event_limit for CEL: measure if smaller batches improve CEL cycle time
./bin/run_tests.sh \
    --akamai-url "https://host.akamai.com" \
    --client-token "..." --client-secret "..." --access-token "..." \
    --duration 30m --interval 1m --initial-interval 1h --event-limit 10000 \
    --akamai-workers 1 --akamai-batch-size 1000
# Expect: CEL cel_processing_time drops (fewer events to evaluate per cycle),
# EPS may improve if cycle time falls below ~5s
```
